summary_one_line: Input must produce a %d-style decimal-integer conversion whose value is negative so the signed-negativity test takes the true branch.

hypotheses.what_input_feature: A '%d'-class (FORMAT_INT, non-CHAR, non-OCTAL, non-HEX, non-UNSIGNED) decimal conversion whose argument value, parsed from the harness input, is < 0 so that p->data.num.as_signed < 0 evaluates true at line 735.
hypotheses.why_winner_satisfies: Winner cmplog seeds carry a structured header (00 01 00 00 00 19 ...) plus '%'-format bytes that drive an INT conversion with a negative operand; the per-role overlay marks the negative-handling block (lines 738-741, 781, 788) as winner-only [W], and the DIVERGENT BRANCHES line 735 shows W=T:5 F:20 while L never takes the true side (T:0).
hypotheses.why_loser_doesnt: Naive loser seeds reach line 735 (the case-FORMAT_INT path is hit, L F:40) but every integer conversion they construct is non-negative, so is_neg stays 0 and the true branch is never taken; naive must blindly mutate bytes into a value whose signed interpretation is negative.
hypotheses.mechanism_attribution: cmplog's I2SRandReplace reads the CmpLogObserver buffer, where the as_signed<0 comparison and the upstream format/parameter CMPs are recorded, and substitutes operand bytes into the input so it constructs a negative-valued %d conversion; naive lacks any CMP introspection and only stumbles onto a negative value by chance, which it managed in only 1/10 trials.

evidence_trail.claim: The blocked true side is a signed-negativity test on a decimal integer conversion.
cmplog resolves the branch in 8/10 trials while naive resolves only 1/10, a decisive I2S delta.
The negative-handling block executed only on winner-resolving seeds, confirming the winner takes the true side.
Winner takes the true side of line 735 (T=5) while loser never does (T=0), reaching only the false side.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: d=1   L 735  T=5 F=20  T=0 F=40  is_neg = (p->data.num.as_signed < (mp_intmax_t)0) ? 1 : 0;  <-- BLOCKER

evidence_trail.claim: Both winner and loser reach the FORMAT_INT case, so the divergence is the value's sign, not the conversion type.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: d=1   L 701  T=60 F=191  T=330 F=403  case FORMAT_INT:

evidence_trail.claim: cmplog adds I2SRandReplace, which scans the input for an operand and substitutes the other operand recorded in the CMP buffer.
evidence_trail.exact_quote:
`I2SRandReplace` reads the post-execution `CmpLogObserver` buffer,
picks a CMP entry, scans the input for byte sequences matching one
operand, and substitutes the other operand at those offsets.

evidence_trail.claim: naive has no I2S capability and only havoc/token mutators.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: **Observed `mutation_op` in seed metadata**: any of the above. No I2S.

mechanism_consistency_check.verification_method: Ran `python3 tools/db_query.py lineage --branch 289 --role W --fuzzer cmplog --trial 1 --seed 6543e87c49a8de08`; the chain reports 'I2S-floor signal: 2 ancestor dash row(s) at depth 12, 13' (mutation_op = -), the ParentInfo-only signature of an I2SRandReplace find in cmplog under the current build.

falsifiability.would_be_refuted_by: A synthetic harness where the negative %d value is fully byte-controlled (no CMP gate on its bytes) yet cmplog still beats naive on this branch, showing the divergence is not driven by I2S operand substitution.

weakest_evidence_point: The byte-diff offsets do not isolate a single literal operand that I2S substitutes to force negativity, so the exact CMP that I2S solves to flip the sign is inferred rather than pinpointed.
